source.title: Fired Heater Tube Rupture and Fire at Marathon Martinez Renewables Facility

context.operator: Marathon Martinez Renewables (subsidiary of Marathon Petroleum Corporation)

event.top_event: Fired Heater Tube Rupture and Loss of Containment
actions_taken: Emergency shutdown initiated within less than one minute of tube rupture
Unit depressurized following tube rupture
Fire extinguished after approximately 15-20 minutes
Injured field operator transported by helicopter to hospital
Post-incident: Valve B removed from startup hydrogen piping
Post-incident: Valve position indicator added to Valve A viewable from control room
Post-incident: Dual diverse check valves installed in startup hydrogen piping
Post-incident: Combustibles analyzer readings made viewable in control room
Post-incident: TDL analyzer installed for continual bridgewall combustibles monitoring
Post-incident: New two-day fired heater safety training class developed and delivered
Post-incident: Warning signs installed at each burner to remove air register plates before lighting
Post-incident: Ignitor panel relocated to same side as air register plates
Post-incident: Operating procedures updated to require removal of air register cover plates before lighting burners
Post-incident: New tube metal temperature operating limits and alarms configured
Post-incident: Pass outlet temperature NTE limit lowered from 1,100 to 850 degrees F
Post-incident: PHA revised to reflect potential for flow diversion through startup piping
Post-incident: Inherently safer systems analysis updated prior to unit restart
Post-incident: Heater tubes replaced with higher design temperature metallurgy
Post-incident: Corporate audit of Martinez facility conducted in early 2024
Post-incident: Marathon made organizational changes to corporate refining team and Martinez facility leadership
event.summary: On November 19, 2023, at 12:21 a.m., a convection section tube ruptured in a fired heater during the initial startup of a renewable diesel hydroprocessing unit at the Marathon Martinez Renewables facility in Martinez, California. The rupture released over 200,000 pounds of renewable diesel and approximately 2,200 pounds of hydrogen, which ignited, causing a fire that seriously injured one field operator with third-degree burns to over 80 percent of his body. The incident was caused by two concurrent conditions: (1) a misaligned manual valve (Valve B, open when it should have been closed) diverted a significant portion of process flow around the fired heater through startup piping located downstream of the safety instrumented system flow meter, defeating the low-flow safety interlock; and (2) two of the four in-service burners were operating with cover plates blocking their air intakes, causing afterburning in the convection section. The combination of low process flow and afterburning caused the convection section tubes to overheat well above their 1,120 degrees F design temperature, reaching up to 1,710 degrees F. Contributing factors included the absence of Not-to-Exceed tube metal temperature limits and alarms, inadequate combustion monitoring available to board operators, poor valve labeling and Walk the Line practices, inadequate operator training on burner operation and startup piping, an ineffective process hazard analysis that failed to identify the reverse-flow diversion scenario, and inadequate corporate oversight of the Martinez facility's conformance to Marathon standards. The incident resulted in approximately $350 million in property damage and the unit was shut down for approximately one year.
recommendations: 2024-01-I-CA-R1: Marathon Martinez Renewables to implement engineering safeguards to detect and prevent afterburning in the fired heater, including combustibles measurements, flame detectors, and/or thermocouples monitorable from the control room.

2024-01-I-CA-R2: Marathon Martinez Renewables to implement tube metal temperature alarming consistent with updated corporate guidance, with predetermined response actions including when to stop troubleshooting and remove personnel from the vicinity of the fired heater.

2024-01-I-CA-R3: Marathon Martinez Renewables to implement changes to improve Walk the Line performance, including pre-startup valve field walkdowns, improved shift communication policies, and Walk the Line training for all management levels.

2024-01-I-CA-R4: Marathon Martinez Renewables to complete a comprehensive gap assessment against Marathon Petroleum Corporation policies covering Operating Limits, Process Hazard Analysis, and PSM/RMP Refining Operating Procedures.

2024-01-I-CA-R5: Marathon Petroleum Corporation to update the corporate Process Heater Not-to-Exceed Limits and Alarms standard with tube metal temperature alarming guidance and predetermined response actions including personnel removal from heater vicinity.

2024-01-I-CA-R6: Marathon Petroleum Corporation to update the corporate Heater Application Standard with requirements for protecting fired heaters from low process flow where piping diverges downstream of a flow meter, and engineering safeguard requirements to detect and prevent afterburning.

2024-01-I-CA-R7: Marathon Petroleum Corporation to confirm results of the Martinez facility gap assessment and conduct an Operations Excellence full assessment, developing and implementing action items.

2024-01-I-CA-R8: API to revise API RP 556 with mandatory requirements for proper response to high tube metal temperatures, design requirements for protecting fired heaters from low process flow downstream of flow meters, and engineering safeguard requirements to detect and prevent afterburning.

hazards.name: High-pressure flammable process fluid (renewable diesel and hydrogen) contained within fired heater tubes at elevated temperature
Fired heater combustion energy capable of overheating process tubes

threats.name: Low process flow through fired heater tubes due to valve misalignment causing flow diversion through startup piping
Afterburning in convection section due to blocked burner air intakes (cover plates on in-service burners)
Inadequate safe operating limits and alarms allowing continued operation beyond unsafe tube metal temperatures

consequences.name: Fired heater tube rupture and release of flammable renewable diesel and hydrogen
Fire engulfing fired heater following ignition of released flammable material
Serious injury to field operator (third-degree burns to over 80% of body)
Major property damage (approximately $350 million) and unit shutdown for approximately one year

controls.name: Safety Instrumented System (SIS) low-flow interlock on fired heater
controls.barrier_role: Automatically shut down all burners upon detection of low process flow to the fired heater
controls.lod_basis: Engineered automatic safety shutdown system designed to bring heater to safe state on low flow detection

supporting_text: The fired heater was equipped with a safety instrumented system (safety interlock) designed to automatically stop the common fuel supply to all burners when process flow to the heater was detected to be lower than the minimum flow requirement.
this safety feature did not prevent the incident because a low process flow condition during unit startup was never detected—either by instrumentation or by unit personnel
the low-flow condition was not detected because the process material was diverted through piping that was downstream of the flow meter
the flow diversion occurred downstream of available flow meters

controls.name: SIS flow meter for low-flow detection
controls.barrier_role: Measure process flow to fired heater and trigger SIS shutdown on low flow
controls.lod_basis: Instrumentation providing input signal to safety instrumented system

supporting_text: The safety instrumented system's flow meter did not detect the low flow to the fired heater, however, because the process material was diverted through piping that was downstream of the flow meter
the flow diversion occurred downstream of available flow meters
the startup piping flow meter was not configured to detect flow in the reverse direction

controls.name: Valve alignment verification and Walk the Line practices
controls.barrier_role: Ensure correct valve alignment before and during startup to prevent unintended flow diversion
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control requiring field walkdowns to verify valve positions before startup

supporting_text: There was no formal effort to verify all valve positions before this second startup attempt, and as a result, personnel did not identify that Valve B was open.
Marathon did not effectively implement the 'Walk the Line' philosophy and did not ensure that valves were correctly aligned before starting up the unit.
Marathon managers would manipulate valves themselves during startup activities, both of which prevented operators from achieving the core Walk the Line tenet
The field operator was not instructed to close Valve B, and Valve B remained open.

controls.name: Valve position indicators for startup hydrogen valves
controls.barrier_role: Provide board operators with real-time visibility of startup valve open/closed status to detect misalignment
controls.lod_basis: Instrumentation providing valve status feedback to control room

supporting_text: Neither of the startup valves was equipped with a position indicator that could be monitored from the control room screen.
Neither Valve A nor Valve B was equipped with position indicators that the board operators could see on the control room display to confirm their alignment
The board operators could have noticed and corrected the valve misalignment, restoring flow to the heater well before the tube rupture, had the open-close status of these valves been visible to them.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) / HAZOP for identifying flow diversion scenarios
controls.barrier_role: Identify hazard scenarios including potential flow diversions that could defeat the SIS and recommend corrective actions
controls.lod_basis: Systematic hazard identification process required by regulation and company standards

supporting_text: Marathon had not identified the potential flow diversion either in process hazard analyses (PHAs) or when specifically looking for flow diversions around fired heaters after a similar 2018 incident at another Marathon refinery.
The PHA team did not realize that material would reverse-flow through the piping, away from the fired heater.
The PHA team did not have sufficient hydraulic analysis information available during the PHA to understand the likely flow directions through the startup piping during all operating modes.
The PHA team did not consider the startup piping to be potential 'bypasses' or 'flow diversions'

controls.name: Operating procedures and operator training for burner lighting (cover plate removal)
controls.barrier_role: Ensure operators remove air intake cover plates before lighting burners to prevent afterburning
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control through written procedures and training to ensure safe burner operation

supporting_text: the operating procedures did not direct operators to remove cover plates before lighting burners

the operating procedure for starting the heater and lighting burners only instructed the board operator to remotely open the air registers and did not instruct the field operator to remove the cover plates

Marathon did not ensure that these operators, who may not have had fired heater safety training and experience prior to working at the Martinez facility, had sufficient knowledge about safe burner operation.
two of the four burners in service (burners 1 and 6) had their air intakes obstructed by cover plates, contributing to poor burner operation and afterburning inside the fired heater

controls.name: Flame detection system on fired heater burners
controls.barrier_role: Detect loss of flame at burners and trigger automatic safety shutdown to prevent dangerous combustion conditions including afterburning
controls.lod_basis: Engineered flame monitoring tied into safety instrumented system

supporting_text: The fired heater involved in the incident was equipped with flame detectors on 4 of the 11 burners
At the time of the incident, only one of four operating burners (burner 1) had a flame detector.
the board operator had no information on the flames of the other three burners and had no way of knowing whether the burners remained lit
The available flame detection capability was ineffective for protecting the fired heater on the night of the incident.
when operations personnel restarted the fired heater, the heater was operating with the safety instrumented function associated with the flame detectors bypassed

controls.name: Combustibles monitoring at bridgewall (available to control room)
controls.barrier_role: Detect incomplete combustion and afterburning conditions inside the fired heater to allow operator intervention
controls.lod_basis: Continuous process monitoring instrumentation providing early warning of combustion anomalies

supporting_text: The fired heater involved in the incident was equipped with a combustibles analyzer at the bridgewall, but its readings were only available at a local panel next to the heater.
there was no continual combustibles monitoring available to the board operators at the control room
the combustibles data was not recorded and therefore was not available for review post-incident
the board operators could not detect incomplete combustion inside the radiant section of the heater that would have been indicative of afterburning leading up to the tube overheat and rupture
There was also insufficient flame detection and combustibles monitoring capability to detect afterburning conditions inside the heater.

controls.name: Tube metal temperature Not-to-Exceed (NTE) limits and alarms
controls.barrier_role: Alert operators when tube metal temperatures exceed safe operating limits and trigger predetermined response actions including heater shutdown and personnel evacuation
controls.lod_basis: Safe operating limit instrumentation and alarm system required by company standards and process safety regulations

supporting_text: Marathon had not equipped the fired heater at the Martinez facility with adequate safe operating limits (Not-to-Exceed limits) and alarms to alert personnel that the tube metal temperatures had exceeded their mechanical design limits and that the fired heater was in an unsafe state.
Marathon Martinez had not established Not-to-Exceed limits, or the limits at which all troubleshooting should end, for the fired heater tube metal temperatures

Marathon did not set Not-to-Exceed limits for the fired heater tube metal temperatures, and as a result, personnel never stopped troubleshooting efforts—and did not shut down the fired heater remotely—before the tube ruptured.
none of the activated alarms required personnel to shut down the fired heater. Rather, per Marathon policies, the alarms that activated were intended to prompt troubleshooting efforts.

controls.name: Remote shutdown of fired heater from control room
controls.barrier_role: Allow board operators to remotely shut down the fired heater to prevent escalation when unsafe conditions are detected, without requiring field operator proximity
controls.lod_basis: Remote emergency shutdown capability as a mitigation to prevent personnel exposure and limit consequence severity

supporting_text: Marathon Martinez facility personnel were unaware of the flow diversion or afterburning and continued to troubleshoot the fired heater operation, even though they had the capability to shut down the heater remotely from the control room.
Marathon Martinez personnel did not shut down the fired heater remotely from the control room, even though they had the capability to do so.
personnel continuing to troubleshoot high tube metal temperatures until the tube ruptured instead of shutting down the fired heater remotely from the control room
The board operators were not able to immediately shut down the heater involved in the incident, because the emergency shutdown valve for the fuel gas remained open due to fire damage.

controls.name: Worker proximity controls and exclusion zones near fired heater during unsafe conditions
controls.barrier_role: Prevent workers from being in close proximity to fired heater when tube temperatures are dangerously high or heater is in unsafe condition
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control to limit personnel exposure to fired heater hazards during abnormal conditions

supporting_text: Marathon did not prevent workers from being in close proximity to fired heaters while tube temperatures were high or the fired heater was otherwise in an unsafe condition, such as by requiring troubleshooting to stop and preventing personnel from approaching the area
personnel directed the field operator to conduct troubleshooting actions in close proximity to the fired heater while the heater was in an unsafe condition (had high tube temperatures)

neither Marathon's corporate standards nor recommended engineering controls and practices issued by the American Petroleum Institute (API) adequately prevent workers from being in close proximity to fired heaters while tube temperatures are dangerously high

In an effort to reduce the tube metal temperatures, Marathon Martinez facility personnel directed a field operator to turn off some of the heater's burners—an operation that placed the field operator immediately next to the fired heater

controls.name: Corporate oversight and standards conformance auditing
controls.barrier_role: Ensure Martinez facility conforms to Marathon corporate safety standards including fired heater protection, PHA, operating limits, and operating procedures
controls.lod_basis: Management system control to verify facility compliance with corporate process safety requirements

supporting_text: Marathon did not adequately ensure that the Martinez facility met the company's minimum safety expectations before it started up as a renewable diesel facility.

the Martinez facility lagged behind in conforming to Marathon's corporate standards, leading to deficiencies in local policies and their implementation that contributed to the November 19, 2023, incident.

Marathon Petroleum Corporation's ineffective oversight of the Martinez facility's conformance to internal corporate standards after 2019 ... led to deficiencies in the facility's management of technology upgrades and local policies that contributed to the November 19, 2023, incident.
at the time of the 2023 incident, the Marathon Martinez facility had not yet performed a full gap assessment against a number of standards, including the Heater Application Standard

controls.name: Equipment labeling and nomenclature consistency with operating procedures
controls.barrier_role: Ensure field equipment labels match operating procedure nomenclature to prevent operator confusion and valve misalignment
controls.lod_basis: Human factors administrative control to reduce likelihood of operator error during valve manipulation

supporting_text: The significant confusion about the names and purposes of the valves on the startup piping arising from discrepancies between the nomenclature used in the operating procedure and the corresponding field labeling likely contributed to the misalignment of one of the startup valves
Marathon did not perform an effective human factors analysis of the renewable diesel unit's equipment or startup procedures.
the new operating procedures still referred to only one 'Taylor Bypass Valve'
Half of them thought the chain valve was the ['Taylor Bypass Valve' referenced in procedures]. Half of them thought the valve at grade was the ['Taylor Bypass Valve' referenced in procedures].

controls.name: Inherently safer systems analysis to eliminate unnecessary valves and piping
controls.barrier_role: Identify and eliminate unnecessary equipment (Valve B and associated startup piping) that introduces hazard potential
controls.lod_basis: Inherently safer design approach required by Contra Costa County Industrial Safety Ordinance

supporting_text: Marathon kept an unnecessary valve (Valve B) and its associated piping that had no design purpose, which introduced confusion and increased the potential for a valve misalignment during startup activities.
Had Marathon implemented an effective inherently safer systems analysis on this unit, the company should have removed the unnecessary valve and piping.
Marathon chose to keep Valve B and its associated piping even though it did not have a design purpose.

The PHA team made a general comment in the inherently safety systems checklist to the effect that although operator actions could result in some process upsets, 'it was verified [during the PHA] that sufficient safeguards exist'—a conclusion that is counter to the purpose of inherently safer design.

controls.name: Emergency response and fire suppression
controls.barrier_role: Respond to fire following tube rupture to extinguish fire and limit injury and property damage
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response procedures and on-site fire response capability

supporting_text: Marathon Martinez operations personnel initiated an emergency shutdown within less than one minute of the tube rupture, depressurized the unit, and responded to the fire.
The fire was mostly extinguished after about 15 to 20 minutes.
An operator had to close the unit's main fuel gas supply to stop fuel gas flow to the heater and stop the fire.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.

pif_id_reference.PIF-001: Inadequate operator training on fired heater burner operation, startup piping, and cover plate removal procedures
pif_id_reference.PIF-002: Operator confusion due to inconsistent equipment labeling and procedure nomenclature for startup valves
pif_id_reference.PIF-003: Managers manipulating valves without communicating to operators, undermining Walk the Line practices
pif_id_reference.PIF-004: PHA team lacked hydraulic analysis data and did not identify reverse-flow diversion scenario
pif_id_reference.PIF-005: Operating procedures did not include step to remove cover plates before lighting burners
pif_id_reference.PIF-006: Absence of Not-to-Exceed alarms meant operators did not recognise heater was in unsafe state requiring shutdown
pif_id_reference.PIF-007: Personnel directed field operator to troubleshoot at heater rather than initiating remote shutdown
pif_id_reference.PIF-008: Inadequate corporate oversight allowed Martinez facility to lag behind Marathon corporate safety standards

pif_detail.competence_mentioned_basis: Multiple operators had only six months of experience; operators lacked prior fired heater safety training; operators did not understand startup piping purpose or valve function
pif_detail.communication_mentioned_basis: Significant confusion about valve names between shifts and between operators and supervisors; infrared scan results miscommunicated; no shift communication about open Valve B
pif_detail.situational_awareness_mentioned_basis: Board operators unaware of flow diversion; personnel unaware of afterburning; no control system data available to alert operators of flow diversion or combustion conditions
pif_detail.procedures_mentioned_basis: Operating procedures did not require cover plate removal before lighting burners; startup procedure did not clearly indicate which valve to open; procedures did not mention Valve B
pif_detail.tools_equipment_mentioned_basis: No valve position indicators on startup valves; combustibles analyzer not connected to control room; inadequate flame detection coverage; SIS flow meter located downstream of diversion point
pif_detail.safety_culture_mentioned_basis: Managers manipulating valves without operator knowledge; facility lagged behind corporate standards; Walk the Line not effectively implemented
pif_detail.management_of_change_mentioned_basis: Marathon did not perform MOOC for transition from terminal to renewable fuels refinery; renewables conversion project did not fully implement corporate heater standards
pif_detail.supervision_mentioned_basis: Managers manipulating valves themselves during startup; inadequate corporate oversight of Martinez facility conformance to standards
pif_detail.training_mentioned_basis: Operators lacked fired heater safety training; no specific training on cover plates; operator training material did not discuss startup piping; new operators had no prior operations experience
